questions.question: Work-energy theorem states that the work done on a body is equal to
questions.explanation: According to work-energy theorem, W = ΔKE.

questions.question: Total mechanical energy of an isolated system remains constant if

options.A: No external force acts on it

questions.explanation: In an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is conserved if no external work is done.

questions.question: Work done by a conservative force depends on
questions.explanation: For conservative forces like gravity or spring, work depends only on initial and final positions.

questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg is lifted to a height of 10 m. Work done against gravity is
Kinetic energy of a body of mass m and velocity v is given by
questions.explanation: KE = 1/2 × mass × velocity².

questions.question: A car of mass 1000 kg moving at 20 m/s comes to rest. Work done by friction is
questions.explanation: Work done = change in KE = 1/2 m v² = 1/2 × 1000 × 400 = 2 × 10⁵ J.

questions.question: A spring stores 50 J of energy when stretched. The work done to stretch it is
questions.explanation: Work done on spring = energy stored = 50 J.

questions.question: If work done by a force is 100 J and displacement is 5 m, magnitude of force along displacement is
questions.explanation: Work = F × d → F = W/d = 100/5 = 20 N.

questions.question: Velocity of a body if its kinetic energy is 200 J and mass is 8 kg is
A 2 kg object is lifted 5 m vertically. Work done = ?
questions.explanation: Work = m × g × h = 2 × 10 × 5 = 100 J. (Check: Use g = 10 m/s²) Corrected → B = 100 J

questions.question: Work done is zero when displacement is zero or force is perpendicular to displacement. Which concept does this explain?
questions.explanation: This is part of the definition of mechanical work.

questions.question: An object is thrown vertically up. At the highest point, its kinetic energy is
questions.explanation: At maximum height, velocity = 0 → KE = 0; PE is maximum.

questions.question: A body of mass 10 kg is moving at 5 m/s. Its kinetic energy is
Which of the following is an example of non-conservative work?
questions.explanation: Friction is a non-conservative force; work depends on path.

questions.question: If a body falls freely from height h, its speed just before hitting the ground is
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² + 2gh; u = 0 → v = √(2gh).

questions.question: A 5 kg object moves at 10 m/s. Power required to stop it in 5 s is
questions.explanation: KE = 1/2 × 5 × 100 = 250 J; Power = Work/Time = 250/0.5? Correct → 250/5 = 50 W. Adjusted answer → A = 50 W

questions.question: Total work done on a body moving in a circle at constant speed is
questions.explanation: Displacement along the direction of centripetal force is zero; W = 0.

questions.question: A body of mass m is projected vertically upward with velocity v. Maximum height reached is
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² + 2as, final v = 0 → h = v² / 2g.

questions.question: Rate of doing work is called power. If 500 J is done in 10 s, power is
questions.explanation: Power = Work / Time = 500 / 10 = 50 W.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg moving at 3 m/s collides and stops. Work done by friction is
questions.explanation: KE lost = work done by friction = 1/2 × 2 × 3² = 9 J.

questions.question: The energy stored in a stretched spring is proportional to
questions.explanation: PE stored in spring = 1/2 k x².

questions.question: Work done by gravitational force is positive when
questions.explanation: Gravity assists motion downward → work positive.

questions.question: Velocity of a body at bottom of incline from height h, ignoring friction, is
questions.explanation: PE at top converts to KE at bottom → v = √(2gh).

questions.question: Mechanical energy of a pendulum at mean position is
questions.explanation: At mean position, velocity is maximum → KE maximum, PE minimum.

questions.question: A body of mass m slides down frictionless incline of height h. Work done by gravity is
questions.explanation: Work done by gravity = loss of potential energy = mgh.
